content.full_text:
California
-
Fair Political
SUPERSEDEt
Practices Commission
[‘lc 2.
1 aX1
February 23,
1995
Tina K. Tucci
District Secretary
Big River Community Services District
P.O.
Box 2376
Big River,
CA
92242
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—95—012
Dear Ms. Tucci:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of LaMarr Mosley and Tom McNeal, directors of the Big River
Community Services District, pursuant to the conflict—of—interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
Since your
advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision,
but instead seeks general guidance,
we are treating your request
as one for informal assistance.
2
Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any
conduct which may have already taken place.
In addition, this
letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The Commission does
not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
In addition,
other laws restrict the ability of public
officials to hold two different public offices simultaneously if
the offices are determined to be “incompatible offices.”
It would be advisable to contact your district counsel or the
Attorney General’s Office with respect to the doctrine of
“incompatible offices” and other provisions of law that might
apply to your facts.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000
- 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—95—012
Page
2
QUESTIONS
1.
Does the Act prevent a public official from holding
multiple public positions within a single public agency or a
position with a business entity?
2.
Does Mr.
LaMarr Mosley’s salary as an employee of the
Big Bear Community Services District disqualify him from
participating in governmental decisions in his capacity as a
director of the district?
3.
Does Mr.
Tom McNeal have a conflict of interest in
district decisions because he is the manager of a recreational
vehicle park which adjoins the district?
CONCLUS IONS
1.
The Act does not prevent a public official from holding
multiple public positions within a single public agency,
or from
holding private employment with a business entity.
2.
Mr. Mosley’s governmental salary from the community
services district does not disqualify him from participating in
governmental decisions in his capacity as a director of the Big
River Community Services District with respect to decisions
affecting the district.
However,
a conflict of interest may exist
where a decision will result in his personal expenses,
income,
assets,
or liabilities or those of his or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing by $250.
3.
Mr.
McNeal may not participate in any governmental
decision which will have a foreseeable and material financial
effect on his economic interests as discussed below.
FACTS
Tom McNeal and LaMarr Mosley are directors of the Big River
Community Services District.
Tom McNeal is employed as the
manager of the Big River Recreational Vehicle Park, which adjoins
the community services district.
The boundaries of the two
properties are separated by a fire road.
Mr.
McNeal’s wife is
also an employee of the same park.
LaMarr Mosley is currently an
employee of the community services district.


Our File No.
1—95-012
Page
3
ANALYSIS
I.
CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making,
or in any way attempting to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to
know he or she has a financial interest.
A director of a couuiiunity services district is a public
official by virtue of his or her membership on the board of
directors of the district.
(Dawson Advice Letter,
No. A-94-180;
Lyon Advice Letter,
No.
A-88—391; Section 82048.)
Therefore,
Mr.
McNeal and Mr. Mosley are public officials for purposes of the
Act.
A.
Holding Public Office
You state in your letter that Mr.
LaMarr Mosley is an
employee of the conuuunity services district and that Mr.
McNeal is
the manager of the Big River Recreational Vehicle Park,
a business
entity.
3
The Act does gj prohibit a public official from holding
multiple public positions, either within a single agency or
different agencies.
(Moore Advice Letter,
No.
1-93-343.)
In
addition, the Act does not prohibit a public official. from holding
private employment.
Therefore, the Act would not prohibit
Mr.
Mosley from serving on the board of directors due to his
employment with the district,
or Mr. McNeal from holding private
employment with a business entity.
B.
Economic Interests
However, the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Act do
prohibit a public official from participating in a decision that
will have a financial effect on his or her economic interests.
This prohibition is applied on a decision—by—decision basis.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
Section 82005 defines “business entity” as an organization or
enterprise operated for profit, including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership,
firm, business trust, joint venture,
syndicate,
corporation or association.


Our File No.
1—95—012
Page 4
from the effect on the public generally,
on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
Section 87103(a),
(c)
and (d).
1.
Director LaMarr Mosley
Goverzimental Salary:
Mr.
Mosley is employed with a local
government agency.
The salary Mr. Mosley receives from the
community services district is not considered income under Section
87103(c)
which could create a conflict of interest for him with
respect to decisions affecting the district.
Section 82030(b) (2),
which defines “income,” provides that salary and reimbursement for
expenses or per diem received from a state, local or federal
government agency is expressly exempted from the definition of
“income” for purposes of the Act.
In addition,
a local government agency is not an organization
or enterprise operated for profit.
It is, therefore not a
“business entity” as defined by the Act.
(Section 82005.)
Thus,
Mr. Mosley’s employment with the community services district will
not result in the district becoming an economic interest of
Mr.
Mosley for purposes of Section 87103(d).
However, Section 87103 states that an official has a
financial interest in a decision if the decision has a material
financial effect on the official or a member of his or her
immediate family.
Thus, the Act provides that a conflict of
interest may exist where a decision will result in a public
official’s personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities or
those of his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing by
$250,
irrespective of the source of the increase or the decrease.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (4); Schreiber Advice Letter,
No.
A—88-459;


Our File No.
1—95-012
Page
5
Torrance Advice Letter,
No. 1-89-142; Fitzpatrick Advice Letter,
No.
A—93-308;
Brown Advice Letter,
No.
A—93—261.)
Absent some other disqualifying financial interest as set
forth in Section 87103, or some direct financial effect on
Mr.
Mosley personally resulting from agovernmental decision as
discussed above, Mr. Mosley will not have conflicts of interest
under the Act due to his position with the district.
2.
Director Tom McNeal
On the basis of the facts you have provided, it appears
Mr.
McNeal has a number of potentially disqualifying economic
interests under the Act.
Employment:
You have indicated that Mr.
McNeal is the
manager of a business entity which is operated as a recreational
vehicle park.
Thus,
Mr. McNeal’s employer, the Big River
Recreational Vehicle Park, is a disqualifying source of income to
him if the income received or promised to him from the park
aggregates $250 or more within the preceding twelve months.
(Section 87103 (c).)
Spouse:
Compensation Mr. McNeal’s spouse receives is also a
potentially disqualifying source of income to him.
Section 82030 provides that the income of an individual also
includes any community property interest in the income of a
spouse.
Therefore,
income to his spouse of $500 or more
(making
his community property interest $250) within the past 12 months,
is a potentially disqualifying economic interest as described in
Section 87103(c).
Business Position:
Finally,
as a manager of the park,
Mr.
McNeal has a disqualifying economic interest within the
meaning of Section 87103(d).
You have provided no facts with respect to governmental
decisions before the district.
However,
Mr.
McNeal may not
participate in any governmental decision that will have a
reasonably foreseeable
4 material financial effect on any of his
economic interests.
If an economic interest of an official will be directly
affected by a governmental decision, Regulation 18702.1
(copy
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular decision.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(In
re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Our File No.
1-95—012
Page
6
attached) provides the appropriate standard for determining
materiality.
Regulations 18702.2
- 18702.6 provide the standards
for determining materiality where an economic interest is
indirectly affected by a governmental decision.
Where a business
will be indirectly affected by a decision, Regulation 18702.2
(copy also attached)
applies.
Therefore,
although the Act does not preclude either
Mr. Mosley or Mr.
McNeal from serving as directors of the Big
River Community Services District, they may not participate in any
governmental decision which will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect on their economic interests as discussed above.
The “Public Generally” Exception
An official may still participate in a decision if the effect
on the official’s economic interest is not distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103, Regulation
18703
(copy attached).)
However,
you have provided no facts to
indicate that this exception would apply to a district decision at
this time.
If you have any questions regarding this matter, please feel
free to contact
me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven C.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
Enclosures

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1.
Does the Act prevent a public official from holding
multiple public positions within a single public agency or a
position with a business entity?
2.
Does Mr.
LaMarr Mosley’s salary as an employee of the
Big Bear Community Services District disqualify him from
participating in governmental decisions in his capacity as a
director of the district?
3.
Does Mr.
Tom McNeal have a conflict of interest in
district decisions because he is the manager of a recreational
vehicle park which adjoins the district?

CONCLUSION: S
1.
The Act does not prevent a public official from holding
multiple public positions within a single public agency,
or from
holding private employment with a business entity.
2.
Mr. Mosley’s governmental salary from the community
services district does not disqualify him from participating in
governmental decisions in his capacity as a director of the Big
River Community Services District with respect to decisions
affecting the district.
However,
a conflict of interest may exist
where a decision will result in his personal expenses,
income,
assets,
or liabilities or those of his or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing by $250.
3.
Mr.
McNeal may not participate in any governmental
decision which will have a foreseeable and material financial
effect on his economic interests as discussed below.
